one_line_takeaway: A broad survey arguing that prior-knowledge (especially symbolic logic and knowledge graphs) and brain-inspired cognitive methods can jointly improve deep learning's adversarial robustness, explainability, and zero/few-shot generalization -- with logic rules the most effective lever for adversarial defense, but with the caveat that knowledge-based defenses can still be reverse-engineered by attackers.
key_finding: Synthesized meta-conclusions (Sec. 6, authors' own analysis of the surveyed corpus): (1) Symbolic/first-order LOGIC rules are the most popular and effective knowledge form across all three problems, and are 'especially useful in combating adversarial attacks' because they let models detect and correct illogical predictions. (2) KNOWLEDGE GRAPHS/ontologies dominate explainability, largely because the domains needing XAI (medical diagnosis, recommenders, VQA) already store data as KGs and KGs are easy to extract explanations from; logic rules and KGs are also effective for zero-shot learning by relating unseen to seen concepts. (3) Probabilistic, physics-informed, and explicit-mathematical representations are the LEAST utilized for these three problems -- physics/math boost accuracy and out-of-distribution robustness but fail to capture the semantic/background context needed to disambiguate subtle cases. (4) Knowledge-informed adversarial defenses are not a silver bullet: if the defense's rationale is known, effective counter-attacks are possible, and work on hiding the defense mechanism is 'practically non-existent.' (5) Cognitive architectures are not built for robustness/zero-shot per se, but their pursuit of general intelligence (dual-process System-1/System-2 hybrids like iCub, IMPACT) implicitly confers these properties.
core_contribution: This is a SURVEY, not an empirical paper, so its contribution is a synthesis/taxonomy rather than any single method or result. The authors (Mumuni & Mumuni) review knowledge-informed and brain-inspired (cognitive) approaches for jointly tackling three deep-learning problems: adversarial robustness, explainability (XAI), and zero-/few-shot generalization. Its self-stated novelty (Sec. 1.5): 'Different from all previous surveys, this paper is particularly focused on knowledge- and cognitive-driven approaches that aim to mitigate problems of adversarial attacks, explainability and zero- or few-shot learning.' The organizing framework is the contribution: (1) a taxonomy of prior-knowledge representation forms (mathematical/physics equations, knowledge graphs & ontologies, symbolic/first-order logic, probabilistic relations, implicit knowledge from foundation models); (2) two families of methods -- prior-knowledge-informed DL (Sec. 3) and cognitive-science-inspired DL, split into cognitive architectures e.g. ACT-R, LIDA, CLARION, iCub, IMPACT and brain-inspired DNNs e.g. CORnet-S aligned to ventral-stream areas V1/V2/V4/IT (Sec. 4); (3) a cross-cutting comparison (Table 1) rating cited methods across four objectives (accuracy, explainability, adversarial robustness, zero/few-shot); and (4) a bidirectional framing that DL is not only improved by neuroscience but also feeds back into cognitive science (Sec. 5, brain decoding / fMRI scene reconstruction from vision-language priors).
threat_model: Descriptive only (survey, not a defense proposal). The adversarial-robustness third of the paper adopts the classic evasion threat model: an attacker adds subtle, human-imperceptible perturbations to a DL classifier's inputs to force wrong predictions -- the paper opens with Szegedy et al.'s AlexNet-on-ImageNet example (a truck misclassified as 'ostrich' with high confidence). It notes attacks span vision and NLP, and that malicious actors can corrupt sensory inputs or interfere with the prediction process. Crucially for the knowledge-based defenses it catalogs (e.g. object co-occurrence checks, logic-rule consistency), the paper stresses that counter-attacks remain possible if the adversary knows the defense's internal logic/data distribution, and warns (Sec. 6b) that future defenses may need to conceal their reasoning ('adversarial smokescreens'). The paper surveys and compares defenses rather than proposing or empirically evaluating a new one.
limitations: (1) As a survey it presents no original evaluation; all quantitative figures are compiled from cited works measured under different datasets/settings, and the authors explicitly caution (Sec. 3.4) that Table 1's accuracy numbers are 'not meant to serve as a basis for comparing the various methods.' (2) The three themes (adversarial robustness, XAI, zero/few-shot) are only loosely unified -- the common thread is 'add prior knowledge / brain inspiration,' which is broad. (3) The paper itself flags open gaps: no unified metrics to compare explanations across heterogeneous representations (NL statements vs. logic vs. concept values); the interpretability-accuracy trade-off remains unresolved and debated; knowledge-informed defenses lack mechanism-concealment; and a large human-vs-AI performance gap persists in challenging situations. (4) Cognitive-architecture claims for robustness/zero-shot are largely argued as implicit/emergent rather than demonstrated. (5) Coverage of concrete deployable security recommendations is thin relative to the conceptual breadth.

datasets_used: No datasets of the authors' own (survey with no original experiments).
ImageNet -- referenced as the setting of the seminal Szegedy et al. adversarial-attack example on AlexNet.
Domains cited as heavy KG/ontology users for XAI: medical diagnosis, recommender systems, visual question answering (illustrative, not benchmarked by the authors).
quantitative_results: All figures below are compiled by the survey FROM CITED WORKS (Table 1), not the authors' own experiments; the paper cautions they are not directly comparable across methods.
CAIPI [128] (XIL / human-in-the-loop): reported accuracy improvement of +37.6%.
CGIntrinsics [178] (physics-based rendering for data augmentation): reported +12.1%.
KGIN [185] (knowledge graph) and CBM [118] (concept bottleneck / HITL): each reported ~+14.5%.

Qualitative meta-result: logic rules are the most frequently used and most effective knowledge form for adversarial robustness; knowledge graphs dominate explainability; probabilistic/physics/math forms are least used for these three problems.
baselines_compared: No experimental baselines (survey). The paper's conceptual contrast is knowledge/cognitive approaches vs. CONVENTIONAL data-driven defenses surveyed in Sec. 2.1: adversarial training, data augmentation, and certified defenses -- whose noted limitations are needing large volumes of adversarial examples, inducing bias that harms clean-sample accuracy, robust overfitting, and vulnerability to counter-attacks / being effective only against attacks they were trained on.
